does: This paper introduces backdoor-powered prompt injection attacks that combine backdoor poisoning with prompt injection to circumvent existing defense mechanisms in large language models. The authors demonstrate that by poisoning supervised fine-tuning data and embedding backdoors into models, attackers can activate triggers that force LLMs to execute injected instructions even when defense strategies like instruction hierarchies are in place. The core contribution is showing that these combined attacks are more potent than standalone prompt injection attacks, as they operate at the model level rather than relying solely on adversarial input manipulation, thereby nullifying defenses designed to prevent prompt injection through instruction-aware processing.
connects: This work builds on two established threat models in LLM security: prompt injection attacks, which exploit LLMs' inability to distinguish between user content and system instructions, and backdoor attacks, which embed malicious behaviors triggered by specific inputs. Recent research has developed instruction hierarchy defenses to mitigate prompt injection by fine-tuning models to respect instruction boundaries. This paper extends that adversarial arms race by showing how backdoor techniques, previously studied in the context of trigger-based model poisoning, can be weaponized specifically to defeat such defenses. The work bridges the intersection of data poisoning vulnerabilities and instruction-following vulnerabilities, demonstrating that defense mechanisms addressing one attack vector remain exposed to hybrid approaches.
enables: This research highlights critical vulnerabilities in fine-tuning-based defenses and motivates development of more robust detection and mitigation strategies. Future work could explore defenses against backdoor-prompt-injection hybrids through anomaly detection, model interpretability techniques to identify backdoor patterns, or fundamental architectural changes to separate instruction processing from content processing. The findings also enable investigation of backdoor resilience in fine-tuning pipelines, potentially leading to certified defenses or data sanitization methods. Additionally, this work suggests that security of LLMs requires simultaneous protection against multiple threat models rather than isolated defense layers, opening directions for unified robustness frameworks and adversarial training approaches tailored to hybrid attacks.
